message.content:
Research configuration inheritance and import patterns in modern tooling.

Focus on:
1. ESLint's extends system:
   - extends: ["eslint:recommended", "./other-config"]
   - How plugins work
   - Override patterns

2. TypeScript's extends:
   - tsconfig.json extends
   - Path resolution

3. Babel's extends and presets

4. CSS @import and cascade

5. Docker's multi-stage inheritance

6. Terraform modules

7. Kubernetes Kustomize overlays

Which system has:
- Best import/extend syntax?
- Clearest override semantics?
- Most flexible inheritance?

How could these patterns apply to "agent rules" that cascade through directories?

Return specific syntax examples and recommendations.

content.text: Vou pesquisar os padrões de herança e importação nas ferramentas modernas de configuração.

input.query: ESLint extends configuration inheritance 2025 flat config
TypeScript tsconfig extends path resolution inheritance

content.content:
Web search results for query: "TypeScript tsconfig extends path resolution inheritance"

Links: [{"title":"TypeScript: TSConfig Option: extends","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html"},{"title":"TypeScript: TSConfig Reference - Docs on every TSConfig option","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/"},{"title":"Resolve tsconfig.json `extends` path using node_modules resolution logic · Issue #18865 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/18865"},{"title":"Add a option of type inheritance from extended tsconfig · Issue #57616 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/57616"},{"title":"tsconfig.json extends field node-style resolution broken · Issue #43245 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/43245"},{"title":"Deep Dive Into Extending tsconfig.json","url":"https://echobind.com/post/deep-dive-into-extending-tsconfig-json"},{"title":"Configuration Inheritance · Issue #9876 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/9876"},{"title":"Module & Module Resolution in Configuration Inheritance · Issue #18834 · microsoft/TypeScript","url":"https://github.com/Microsoft/TypeScript/issues/18834"},{"title":"TypeScript: TSConfig Option: paths","url":"https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/paths.html"},{"title":"vite-tsconfig-paths - npm","url":"https://www.npmjs.com/package/vite-tsconfig-paths"}]

Here's what I found about TypeScript tsconfig `extends` path resolution and inheritance:

## Key Concepts

### The `extends` Property

The value of `extends` is a string which contains a path to another configuration file to inherit from. The path may use Node.js style resolution. The configuration from the base file are loaded first, then overridden by those in the inheriting config file.

### Path Resolution Behavior

The problem is the paths in a tsconfig.json are relative to the folder that that specific tsconfig.json file is in, and not the tsconfig.json doing the extending. So in this example, all the paths in packages/tsconfig.json are relative to the packages/ folder, even when packages/tsconfig.json is extended (i.e., extending doesn't change the folder that paths are relative to).(no content)Relative paths found in the configuration file will be resolved relative to the configuration file they originated in.

### Inheritance Behavior

It's worth noting that when you extend a tsconfig.json, the properties in compilerOptions are merged. And when both files define the same property, the child tsconfig.json wins. However, this is not the case with include and exclude. If you define them in the child tsconfig.json, that exact value will be used; it won't be merged with the value from the base tsconfig.json.(no content)Top-level members other than compilerOptions are completely overridden by following definitions. compilerOptions at the top level is merged (though its individual keys will still be overridden as though they were top-level keys).

### The `paths` Option

A series of entries which re-map imports to lookup locations relative to the baseUrl if set, or to the tsconfig file itself otherwise.(no content)Currently, when a package's tsconfig.json extends the root tsconfig, any paths defined in the package's tsconfig overwrite those from the base. This behavior requires developers to replicate the base paths in every package's tsconfig.

### Practical Implications

That means we always have to define baseUrl, rootDir, outDir, include, and exclude in the child tsconfig.json (the one that extends the base tsconfig.json).(no content)In a monorepo setup, managing shared and package-specific paths efficiently without duplication becomes challenging. Reducing configuration overhead and potential for errors by not having to duplicate shared paths across multiple tsconfig.json files is a common goal.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Kubernetes Kustomize overlays inheritance patterns 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Overlays - KodeKloud Notes","url":"https://notes.kodekloud.com/docs/Certified-Kubernetes-Application-Developer-CKAD/2025-Updates-Kustomize-Basics/Overlays"},{"title":"Declarative Management of Kubernetes Objects Using Kustomize | Kubernetes","url":"https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/manage-kubernetes-objects/kustomization/"},{"title":"Kustomize - Argo CD - Declarative GitOps CD for Kubernetes","url":"https://argo-cd.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user-guide/kustomize/"},{"title":"Kubernetes Kustomize Tutorial (Comprehensive Guide)","url":"https://devopscube.com/kustomize-tutorial/"},{"title":"Helm vs Kustomize in 2025: Patterns, Pros, Cons, and How to Combine Them | justinpolidori.com","url":"https://justinpolidori.com/posts/20250815_helm_kustomize/"},{"title":"What is Kustomize? How to Simplify K8s Deployments","url":"https://www.plural.sh/blog/what-is-kustomize-guide/"},{"title":"Kustomize Tutorial With Instructions & Examples","url":"https://www.densify.com/kubernetes-tools/kustomize/"},{"title":"Components - KodeKloud Notes","url":"https://notes.kodekloud.com/docs/Certified-Kubernetes-Application-Developer-CKAD/2025-Updates-Kustomize-Basics/Components"},{"title":"Support for mixins · Issue #759 · kubernetes-sigs/kustomize","url":"https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/kustomize/issues/759"},{"title":"Kustomize Tutorial: How to Create a K8s App from Multiple Pieces | Mirantis","url":"https://www.mirantis.com/blog/introduction-to-kustomize-part-2-overriding-values-with-overlays"}]

Here are the search results for Kubernetes Kustomize overlays inheritance patterns in 2025:

## Key Concepts

### Base and Overlay Pattern
Kustomize has two key concepts: Base and Overlays. With Kustomize you can reuse the base files (common YAMLs) across all environments and overlay (patches) specifications for each of those environments.(no content)Kustomize's base-and-overlay model eliminates the need to copy YAML across environments. A base defines the canonical, environment-agnostic configuration for an application. Overlays layer on only what differs—replica counts, resource limits, image tags, or configuration values—for development, staging, and production.

### Inheritance Structure
This structure enforces a single source of truth. All environments inherit from the same foundation, dramatically reducing configuration drift. For teams managing many clusters or tenants, this also scales cleanly: adding a new environment typically means creating a new overlay, not reworking existing manifests.

### Typical Project Structure
A typical Kustomize project is organized into two main sections: Base Configuration (contains the shared Kubernetes configurations) and Overlay Directories (contains environment-specific folders where you can adjust or extend the base configuration using patches or by adding new resources).

### Components for Reusable Features
Components let you define reusable configuration logic that can be imported into multiple overlays. This approach is ideal when your application supports several optional features that should only be enabled in specific overlays.(no content)By isolating the configuration logic into components, overlays that require specific features—such as caching or an external database—can simply import the corresponding component. This method minimizes duplicate code and reduces the chance of error.

## 2025 Patterns: Helm + Kustomize Integration

The idea: Use Helm to package the application (templates, defaults, logic) and Kustomize for environment specific tweaks.

**Three patterns identified:**
- Pattern 1 = Kustomize controls a single app's deployment per env. Helm is a sub-step.
- Pattern 2 = Helm controls the release. Kustomize only edits the final YAML.
- Pattern 3 = Kustomize controls the entire environment, including multiple charts and raw manifests, all rendered and applied together.

### GitOps Benefits
Kustomize helps prevent configuration drift by making your Git repository the definitive source of truth for your Kubernetes manifests. Instead of making manual changes directly to a cluster with kubectl, all modifications are defined as patches within version-controlled overlays. This ensures every change is reviewed, approved, and logged through a pull request.

### Best Practices
Keeping base resources, overlays, and patches in separate directories helps maintain clarity and separation between different configurations. Try to keep the common values like namespace and common metadata in the base file.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "ESLint extends configuration inheritance 2025 flat config"

Links: [{"title":"Evolving flat config with extends - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/"},{"title":"Configuration Migration Guide - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/migration-guide"},{"title":"Configuration Files - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/configure/configuration-files"},{"title":"Change Request: Flat config extends · Issue #19116 · eslint/eslint","url":"https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/19116"},{"title":"Change Request: Make it easier to inherit flat configs from the repo root · Issue #18385 · eslint/eslint","url":"https://github.com/eslint/eslint/issues/18385"},{"title":"How to use new \"flat config\" approach in Eslint? · vercel/next.js · Discussion #49337","url":"https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/49337"},{"title":"ESLint's new config system, Part 2: Introduction to flat config - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/blog/2022/08/new-config-system-part-2/"},{"title":"Plugin Migration to Flat Config - ESLint - Pluggable JavaScript Linter","url":"https://eslint.org/docs/latest/extend/plugin-migration-flat-config"},{"title":"Configuration: ESLint | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/eslint"},{"title":"ESLint 9 Flat config tutorial - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/aolyang/eslint-9-flat-config-tutorial-2bm5"}]

Here are the search results for ESLint's `extends` configuration inheritance in flat config (2025):

## Key Updates

Your eslint.config.js files can now use extends to simplify your configuration.(no content)When ESLint v9.0.0 was released in April 2024, they enabled the new configuration system by default, knowing there would be a period of challenges as the ecosystem switched over.

### The Problem with Flat Config

Whereas eslintrc had extends, flat config forces folks to insert objects into the export array, sometimes using the spread (...) syntax and sometimes not. For beginners, the spread syntax is confusing, and for everyone, some plugins export config objects while others export config arrays, so you never know whether or not to use the spread operator.

### The Solution: `defineConfig()` with `extends`

Ultimately, ESLint realized that the best way to solve this set of problems was to reintroduce extends. The defineConfig() function allows you to specify an extends array in any object, and that array can contain objects, arrays, or strings (for plugin configs that follow the recommended approach).(no content)A configuration object uses extends to inherit all the traits of another configuration object or array (including rules, plugins, and language options) and can then modify all the options.

### How It Works

This flattening behavior is designed to eliminate some of the confusion around the use of the spread operator (...) with the new configuration system. With defineConfig(), you never need to use the spread operator (unless you really want to!).(no content)In flat configs, extends will not trigger any file loading. Instead, it will allow string identifiers for configs contained in plugins as well as objects.

### Migration from eslintrc

A key difference is that eslintrc files use string-based import system inside the plugins property to load plugins and inside the extends property to load external configurations. Flat config files represent plugins and parsers as JavaScript objects. This means you can use CommonJS require() or ES module import statements to load plugins and custom parsers from external files.

### Using `extends` in Flat Config

Use `extends` for reusing configurations - when you want to inherit and build upon existing configurations from plugins, shareable packages, or predefined configs. It's suitable for plugin configuration when applying recommended or specific configurations from ESLint plugins (e.g., example/recommended), for shareable configurations when using npm packages that export configuration objects (e.g., eslint-config-example), and for predefined configurations when using ESLint's built-in configurations like js/recommended or js/all.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Terraform modules composition inheritance patterns"

Links: [{"title":"Module Composition | Terraform | HashiCorp Developer","url":"https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/composition"},{"title":"Providers Within Modules - Configuration Language | Terraform | HashiCorp Developer","url":"https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/providers"},{"title":"[Feature] Module Variable and Output Inheritance (or simular) · Issue #31485 · hashicorp/terraform","url":"https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform/issues/31485"},{"title":"Terraform Module Registry – Hierarchical Inheritance","url":"https://scalr.com/blog/hierarchical-terraform-module-registry"},{"title":"module inheritance","url":"https://groups.google.com/g/terraform-tool/c/JMZHd9ppDpg/m/i1dK_5xvXPkJ"},{"title":"Provider inheritance in modules · Issue #3285 · hashicorp/terraform","url":"https://github.com/hashicorp/terraform/issues/3285"},{"title":"Best practices for code base structure and organization - AWS Prescriptive Guidance","url":"https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/terraform-aws-provider-best-practices/structure.html"},{"title":"Module creation - recommended pattern | Terraform | HashiCorp Developer","url":"https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/tutorials/modules/pattern-module-creation"},{"title":"Simplifying Provider Inheritance in Terraform with configuration_aliases | by Sampark Mehrotra | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@sampark02/simplifying-provider-inheritance-in-terraform-with-configuration-aliases-ca9e07fa8346"},{"title":"Terraform Inheritance/Overrides - Terraform - HashiCorp Discuss","url":"https://discuss.hashicorp.com/t/terraform-inheritance-overrides/12832"}]

Here are the search results for Terraform modules composition inheritance patterns:

## Module Composition

HashiCorp calls this flat style of module usage "module composition" because it takes multiple composable building-block modules and assembles them together to produce a larger system.(no content)Instead of a module embedding its dependencies, creating and managing its own copy, the module receives its dependencies from the root module, which can therefore connect the same modules in different ways to produce different results.

### Dependency Inversion Pattern

Rather than trying to write a module that itself tries to detect whether something exists and create it if not, Terraform recommends applying the dependency inversion approach: making the module accept the object it needs as an argument, via an input variable.(no content)This is consistent with Terraform's declarative style: rather than creating modules with complex conditional branches, we directly describe what should already exist and what we want Terraform to manage itself. By following this pattern, we can be explicit about in which situations we expect resources to already be present and which we don't.

## Provider Inheritance

For convenience in simple configurations, a child module automatically inherits default provider configurations from its parent. This means that explicit provider blocks appear only in the root module, and downstream modules can simply declare resources.(no content)Providers can be passed down to descendant modules in two ways: either implicitly through inheritance, or explicitly via the providers argument within a module block.(no content)If you have three modules: root => child => grandchild, then AWS configured in root will affect both child and grandchild. As such, the configuration is "inherited". But if you have a module tree: root => {child, sibling}, then having a configuration in child will not configure the sibling. It only flows downwards.

### Configuration Aliases (Terraform 1.3+)

Modules can now declare which aliases they need using `configuration_aliases`. You can use `provider = aws.east` in your resources, but you don't need to re-declare the provider block inside the module, resulting in cleaner code.

## Best Practices for Module Structure

When you nest modules in subdirectories, avoid going more than one or two levels deep. Deeply nested inheritance structures complicate configurations and troubleshooting. Modules should build on other modules―not build tunnels through them.(no content)Although shared modules inherit providers from calling modules, modules should not configure provider settings themselves. Avoid specifying provider configuration blocks in modules. This configuration should only be declared once globally.(no content)Modules should be opinionated and designed to do one thing well.(no content)For every resource that's defined in a reusable module, include at least one output that references the resource. Variables and outputs let you infer dependencies between modules and resources. Without any outputs, users cannot properly order your module in relation to their Terraform configurations.

## Multi-Cloud Abstractions

Through composition of Terraform modules it is possible to create your own lightweight multi-cloud abstractions by making your own tradeoffs about which platform features are important to you. Opportunities for such abstractions arise in any situation where multiple vendors implement the same concept, protocol, or open standard.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Babel config extends presets inheritance 2025"

Links: [{"title":"Config extends with Plugins and preset-env · Issue #6861 · babel/babel","url":"https://github.com/babel/babel/issues/6861"},{"title":"Guides: Babel | Next.js","url":"https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/guides/babel"},{"title":"Configure Babel · Babel","url":"https://babeljs.io/docs/configuration"},{"title":"Configuring Babel with Encore (Symfony Docs)","url":"https://symfony.com/doc/current/frontend/encore/babel.html"},{"title":"Presets · Babel","url":"https://babeljs.io/docs/presets"},{"title":"How to Structure Your Babel Config JS for Maximum Clarity - Tips and Best Practices","url":"https://moldstud.com/articles/p-how-to-structure-your-babel-config-js-for-maximum-clarity-tips-and-best-practices"},{"title":"Config Files · Babel","url":"https://babeljs.io/docs/config-files"},{"title":"Inheritance issue when subclassing transpiled ES6 class in ES5 · Issue #11749 · babel/babel","url":"https://github.com/babel/babel/issues/11749"},{"title":"@wordpress/babel-preset-default – Block Editor Handbook | Developer.WordPress.org","url":"https://developer.wordpress.org/block-editor/reference-guides/packages/packages-babel-preset-default/"},{"title":"babel.config.js - Expo Documentation","url":"https://docs.expo.dev/versions/latest/config/babel/"}]

Here are the search results for "Babel config extends presets inheritance 2025":

## Babel Configuration: Extends, Presets & Inheritance

### Configuration Merging Behavior

Babel's configuration merging is relatively straightforward. Options will overwrite existing options when they are present and their value is not undefined.(no content)There are, however, a few special cases: For assumptions, parserOpts and generatorOpts, objects are merged, rather than replaced. For plugins and presets, they are replaced based on the identity of the plugin/preset object/function itself combined with the name of the entry.

### Using `extends` in Config Files

In monorepo structures where config is across a package boundary, one alternative would be to create a .babelrc in each sub-package that uses "extends". Unfortunately, this approach can be a bit repetitive, and depending on how Babel is being used, could require setting "babelrcRoots".

### Known Issues with Extends

There's a documented issue on GitHub regarding the `extends` option:

When using `extends` with `@babel/preset-env`, plugins from the extended config may fail to load properly. For example, `@babel/plugin-proposal-class-properties` fails to get loaded from an extending config when using `@babel/preset-env`. If you remove `@babel/preset-env` from the main config, everything works.

### Preset Ordering

Preset ordering is reversed (last to first). This was mostly for ensuring backwards compatibility, since most users listed "es2015" before "stage-0".

### Best Practices for Configuration Inheritance

Leverage the power of Babel's configuration inheritance to simplify your setup. You can create a base config file with common settings and extend it in environment-specific configurations.(no content)Adopt a modular approach: Move large plugin and preset lists to separate files, then require them into the main configuration. This single step can decrease repeated code by up to 45% in medium-sized projects.

### Framework-Specific Approaches

**Next.js:** To start, you only need to define a .babelrc file (or babel.config.js) in the root directory of your project. If such a file is found, it will be considered as the source of truth, and therefore it needs to define what Next.js needs as well, which is the next/babel preset.

**WordPress:** The @wordpress/babel-preset-default is an opinionated configuration. If you would like to add to or change this configuration, you can do so by expanding your Babel configuration to include plugins or presets which override those included through this preset.

**Expo:** babel-preset-expo is the default preset used in Expo projects. It extends the default React Native preset (@react-native/babel-preset) and adds support for decorators, tree-shaking web libraries, and loading font icons.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "Docker multi-stage build inheritance FROM patterns"

Links: [{"title":"Multi-stage | Docker Docs","url":"https://docs.docker.com/build/building/multi-stage/"},{"title":"Multi-stage builds | Docker Docs","url":"https://docs.docker.com/get-started/docker-concepts/building-images/multi-stage-builds/"},{"title":"Advanced multi-stage build patterns | by Tõnis Tiigi | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@tonistiigi/advanced-multi-stage-build-patterns-6f741b852fae"},{"title":"Advanced Dockerfiles: Faster Builds and Smaller Images Using BuildKit and Multistage Builds | Docker","url":"https://www.docker.com/blog/advanced-dockerfiles-faster-builds-and-smaller-images-using-buildkit-and-multistage-builds/"},{"title":"Understanding Docker Multistage Builds - Earthly Blog","url":"https://earthly.dev/blog/docker-multistage/"},{"title":"Multi-stage Docker Builds - DockerBuild.com","url":"https://dockerbuild.com/tutorials/multi-stage-builds"},{"title":"Docker multi-stage build. An effective strategy to build production ready docker images. | by Oluochodhiambo | Medium","url":"https://oluoch-odhiambo.medium.com/docker-multi-stage-build-an-effective-strategy-to-building-production-ready-docker-images-59fda6e94e0"},{"title":"Docker multi-stage builds explained","url":"https://depot.dev/blog/docker-multi-stage-builds"},{"title":"How to Build Smaller Container Images: Docker Multi-Stage Builds | iximiuz Labs","url":"https://labs.iximiuz.com/tutorials/docker-multi-stage-builds"},{"title":"Builder pattern vs. Multi-stage builds in Docker","url":"https://blog.alexellis.io/mutli-stage-docker-builds/"}]

Here's what I found about Docker multi-stage build inheritance FROM patterns:

## Overview

With multi-stage builds, you use multiple FROM statements in your Dockerfile. Each FROM instruction can use a different base, and each of them begins a new stage of the build.(no content)You can selectively copy artifacts from one stage to another, leaving behind everything you don't want in the final image.

## Key Inheritance Patterns

### 1. Using Previous Stages as Base Images

FROM command and --from flag have much more in common and it is not accidental that they are named the same. They both take the same argument, resolve it and then either start a new stage from that point or use it as a source for file copy. That means that same way as you can use --from=stagename you can also use FROM stagename to use a previous stage as a source image for your current stage.(no content)This is useful when multiple commands in the Dockerfile share the same common parts. It makes the shared code smaller and easier to maintain while keeping the child stages separate so that when one is rebuilt it doesn't invalidate the build cache for the others.

### 2. Parallel Builds from Common Base

In BuildKit, the second and third stage in this example would be built concurrently. Example pattern:
```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu AS base
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install git

FROM base AS src1
RUN git clone …

FROM base AS src2
RUN git clone …
```

### 3. Conditional/Branch Pattern with ARG

To have the same behavior with current multistage builds you would need to define different branch conditions as separate stages and use an argument to pick the correct dependency path. The last stage in this Dockerfile is based on after-condition stage that is an alias to an image that is resolved by BUILD_VERSION build argument. Depending on the value of BUILD_VERSION, a different middle section stage is picked.

```dockerfile
ARG BUILD_VERSION=1
FROM alpine AS base
RUN …

FROM base AS branch-version-1
RUN touch version1

FROM base AS branch-version-2
RUN touch version2

FROM branch-version-${BUILD_VERSION} AS after-condition
```

### 4. Image Aliasing Pattern

A build stage doesn't need to contain any commands — it may just be a single FROM line. When you are using an image in multiple places this can be useful to improve readability and making sure that when a shared image needs to be updated, only a single line needs to be changed. In this example, any place that uses image alpine is actually fixed to alpine:3.6 not alpine:latest. When it comes time to update to alpine:3.7, only a single line needs to be changed.

```dockerfile
FROM alpine:3.6 AS alpine
FROM alpine
RUN …
FROM alpine
RUN …
```

## BuildKit Optimizations

BuildKit only builds the stages that the target stage depends on. With BuildKit enabled, building the stage2 target in this Dockerfile means only base and stage2 are processed. There is no dependency on stage1, so it's skipped.(no content)Note that only BuildKit based builders can skip the unused branches. In previous builders all stages would be still built, but their results would be discarded before creating the final image.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

### 1. Using Previous Stages as Base Images

```dockerfile
RUN …
```

There is no dependency on stage1, so it's skipped.(no content)Note that only BuildKit based builders can skip the unused branches. content.text: Agora vou ler exemplos concretos de sintaxe e consolidar as descobertas:

input.prompt: Extract the exact syntax examples for extends in ESLint flat config with defineConfig. Show code examples of how extends works.
Extract the key patterns for Terraform module composition - dependency inversion, flat modules, and how modules receive dependencies. Show code examples.

input.content:
# Research: Configuration Inheritance Patterns for Agent Rules

## Executive Summary

This research analyzes inheritance/import patterns across 7 major configuration systems to identify the best approach for "agent rules" that cascade through directories.

---

## 1. ESLint Flat Config (2025)

### Syntax
```javascript
import { defineConfig } from "eslint/config";
import js from "@eslint/js";

export default defineConfig({
  files: ["**/*.js"],
  extends: [
    "js/recommended",           // String reference
    pluginConfig,               // Object
    [...arrayOfConfigs]         // Array spread handled automatically
  ],
  rules: {
    // Override specific rules
    "no-console": "warn"
  }
});
```

### Strengths
- `defineConfig()` eliminates spread operator confusion
- `extends` accepts strings, objects, OR arrays uniformly
- File-scoped inheritance with `files: ["pattern"]`
- Plugins can export named configs: `"plugin/recommended"`

### Override Semantics
- Later items in `extends` array override earlier ones
- Local `rules` override extended rules
- Clean merge: no complex resolution algorithm

### Weakness
- Requires import statements (no URL-based loading)
- No directory-based auto-discovery

---

## 2. TypeScript tsconfig.json

### Syntax
```json
{
  "extends": "./base/tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "./dist"
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*"]
}
```

### Multi-extends (TypeScript 5.0+)
```json
{
  "extends": [
    "@tsconfig/node18/tsconfig.json",
    "./tsconfig.paths.json"
  ]
}
```

### Strengths
- Simple single-key `extends`
- Node-style resolution for packages
- Multiple extends with array syntax

### Override Semantics
- `compilerOptions`: deep merge (child wins on conflict)
- `include`/`exclude`: complete replacement (no merge)
- Paths resolved relative to originating file

### Weakness
- No override for specific paths
- Complete replacement of arrays is surprising
- No conditional inheritance

---

## 3. Kubernetes Kustomize

### Structure
```
base/
  kustomization.yaml
  deployment.yaml
overlays/
  dev/
    kustomization.yaml    # references ../base
  prod/
    kustomization.yaml    # references ../base
```

### Syntax
```yaml
# overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

resources:
  - ../../base            # Inherit from base

patches:
  - path: increase-replicas.yaml
    target:
      kind: Deployment

components:
  - ../../components/caching   # Reusable feature modules
```

### Strengths
- Explicit base/overlay separation
- Patches are surgical (JSON merge patch, strategic merge)
- Components for cross-cutting features
- Directory-based organization is first-class

### Override Semantics
- Base resources loaded first
- Patches applied in order
- Components can be mixed into any overlay

### Weakness
- Verbose for simple overrides
- Learning curve for patch types
- No inheritance from parent directories (only explicit references)

---

## 4. Terraform Modules

### Syntax
```hcl
module "network" {
  source = "./modules/network"

  # Explicit inputs (dependency inversion)
  vpc_cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

module "app" {
  source = "./modules/app"

  # Wire outputs to inputs
  vpc_id = module.network.vpc_id
}
```

### Strengths
- Dependency inversion: modules don't embed dependencies
- Explicit data flow via inputs/outputs
- Composition over inheritance
- Flat hierarchy recommended

### Override Semantics
- No implicit inheritance
- All configuration is explicit
- Provider inheritance flows downward

### Weakness
- No "extends" concept
- Verbose for simple cases
- Not applicable for cascading rules

---

## 5. Babel Configuration

### Syntax
```javascript
// babel.config.js
module.exports = {
  extends: "./base.babelrc.js",
  presets: [
    ["@babel/preset-env", { targets: "defaults" }]
  ],
  plugins: [
    "@babel/plugin-transform-runtime"
  ]
};
```

### Strengths
- Simple `extends` with path
- Presets bundle common plugins

### Override Semantics
- Objects merged (not replaced)
- Plugins/presets replaced by identity
- Preset order is reversed (last-to-first)

### Weakness
- Reversed preset order is confusing
- Identity-based replacement is complex
- Known bugs with `extends` + `preset-env`

---

## 6. CSS Cascade Layers

### Syntax
```css
/* Establish layer order first */
@layer reset, framework, components, utilities;

/* Import into named layers */
@import url("normalize.css") layer(reset);
@import url("bootstrap.css") layer(framework);

/* Local overrides in higher-priority layer */
@layer utilities {
  .btn { /* Always wins over framework */ }
}
```

### Nested Layers
```css
@import url("theme.css") layer(framework.themes.dark);
```

### Strengths
- Explicit priority declaration upfront
- Layer order controls cascade (not specificity)
- Dot notation for nesting
- Anonymous layers for isolation

### Override Semantics
- First declared = lowest priority
- Last declared = highest priority (except `!important`)
- Unlayered styles beat all layers

### Weakness
- Browser-only (not general config)
- Must declare order before use
- No directory-based organization

---

## 7. Docker Multi-Stage Builds

### Syntax
```dockerfile
FROM node:20 AS base
RUN npm install

FROM base AS development
CMD ["npm", "run", "dev"]

FROM base AS production
RUN npm run build
CMD ["npm", "start"]
```

### Stage Aliasing
```dockerfile
ARG TARGET=production
FROM alpine:3.18 AS alpine

FROM alpine AS builder
RUN apk add build-base

FROM ${TARGET} AS final
```

### Strengths
- Explicit stage naming with `AS`
- Parallel builds from common base
- ARG-based conditional paths
- COPY --from=stage for artifacts

### Override Semantics
- Each FROM starts fresh
- Only COPY brings artifacts forward
- Conditional via ARG substitution

### Weakness
- Linear inheritance only
- No merge semantics
- Domain-specific (container builds)

---

## Comparative Analysis

| System | Import Syntax | Override Clarity | Directory Support | Flexibility |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| ESLint | `extends: []` | High | Low | High |
| TypeScript | `extends: ""` | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Kustomize | `resources: []` | High | High | High |
| Terraform | `module {}` | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| Babel | `extends: ""` | Low | Low | Low |
| CSS Layers | `@layer` | Very High | N/A | Medium |
| Docker | `FROM AS` | High | N/A | Low |

---

## Recommendations for Agent Rules

### Best Import/Extend Syntax: ESLint's `defineConfig`

```javascript
// Why: Uniform handling of strings, objects, arrays
// No spread operator ambiguity
// File-pattern scoping built-in

defineConfig({
  files: ["src/**"],
  extends: ["./base-rules", localRules, plugin.recommended]
})
```

### Clearest Override Semantics: CSS Cascade Layers

```css
/* Why: Explicit priority declaration upfront */
@layer base, project, user;

/* Order is explicit, not implicit */
```

### Most Flexible Inheritance: Kustomize

```yaml
# Why: Directory-based, surgical patches, components
resources:
  - ../../base
patches:
  - path: override.yaml
components:
  - ../shared-features
```

---

## Proposed Design for Agent Rules

### Directory Structure
```
.claude/
  rules/
    __base__.md           # Global base rules
    security/
      __rules__.md        # Security-specific rules
    frontend/
      __rules__.md        # Frontend-specific rules
      react/
        __rules__.md      # React-specific (inherits from frontend)
```

### Inheritance Syntax (Hybrid Approach)

```yaml
# .claude/rules/frontend/react/__rules__.md
---
extends:
  - ../../__rules__.md    # Inherit from frontend
  - @claude/react         # Built-in preset
priority: 100             # Explicit layer priority
scope:
  files: ["**/*.tsx", "**/*.jsx"]
---

# Rules content in markdown...
```

### Key Design Decisions

1. **From ESLint**: `extends` array with mixed sources
2. **From CSS Layers**: Explicit `priority` number
3. **From Kustomize**: Directory-based organization with `__rules__.md`
4. **From TypeScript**: Node-style resolution for packages

### Override Algorithm

```
1. Collect all __rules__.md from root to current directory
2. Sort by priority (lower = base, higher = override)
3. Merge rules: later wins on conflict
4. Apply file scope filtering
```

### Example Resolution

```
Working on: src/components/Button.tsx

Rules loaded (in order):
1. .claude/rules/__base__.md (priority: 0)
2. .claude/rules/frontend/__rules__.md (priority: 50)
3. .claude/rules/frontend/react/__rules__.md (priority: 100)

Final rules = merge(base, frontend, react)
```

---

## Sources

- [ESLint Flat Config Extends](https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/)
- [TypeScript tsconfig extends](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html)
- [Kustomize Overlays](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/manage-kubernetes-objects/kustomization/)
- [Terraform Module Composition](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/composition)
- [Babel Configuration](https://babeljs.io/docs/configuration)
- [CSS Cascade Layers](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/At-rules/@layer)
- [Docker Multi-stage Builds](https://docs.docker.com/build/building/multi-stage/)

# Research: Configuration Inheritance Patterns for Agent Rules

## Executive Summary

This research analyzes inheritance/import patterns across 7 major configuration systems to identify the best approach for "agent rules" that cascade through directories.

---

## 1. ESLint Flat Config (2025)

### Syntax
```javascript
import { defineConfig } from "eslint/config";
import js from "@eslint/js";

export default defineConfig({
  files: ["**/*.js"],
  extends: [
    "js/recommended",           // String reference
    pluginConfig,               // Object
    [...arrayOfConfigs]         // Array spread handled automatically
  ],
  rules: {
    // Override specific rules
    "no-console": "warn"
  }
});
```

### Strengths
- `defineConfig()` eliminates spread operator confusion
- `extends` accepts strings, objects, OR arrays uniformly
- File-scoped inheritance with `files: ["pattern"]`
- Plugins can export named configs: `"plugin/recommended"`

### Override Semantics
- Later items in `extends` array override earlier ones
- Local `rules` override extended rules
- Clean merge: no complex resolution algorithm

### Weakness
- Requires import statements (no URL-based loading)
- No directory-based auto-discovery

---

## 2. TypeScript tsconfig.json

### Syntax
```json
{
  "extends": "./base/tsconfig.json",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "outDir": "./dist"
  },
  "include": ["src/**/*"]
}
```

### Multi-extends (TypeScript 5.0+)
```json
{
  "extends": [
    "@tsconfig/node18/tsconfig.json",
    "./tsconfig.paths.json"
  ]
}
```

### Strengths
- Simple single-key `extends`
- Node-style resolution for packages
- Multiple extends with array syntax

### Override Semantics
- `compilerOptions`: deep merge (child wins on conflict)
- `include`/`exclude`: complete replacement (no merge)
- Paths resolved relative to originating file

### Weakness
- No override for specific paths
- Complete replacement of arrays is surprising
- No conditional inheritance

---

## 3. Kubernetes Kustomize

### Structure
```
base/
  kustomization.yaml
  deployment.yaml
overlays/
  dev/
    kustomization.yaml    # references ../base
  prod/
    kustomization.yaml    # references ../base
```

### Syntax
```yaml
# overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization

resources:
  - ../../base            # Inherit from base

patches:
  - path: increase-replicas.yaml
    target:
      kind: Deployment

components:
  - ../../components/caching   # Reusable feature modules
```

### Strengths
- Explicit base/overlay separation
- Patches are surgical (JSON merge patch, strategic merge)
- Components for cross-cutting features
- Directory-based organization is first-class

### Override Semantics
- Base resources loaded first
- Patches applied in order
- Components can be mixed into any overlay

### Weakness
- Verbose for simple overrides
- Learning curve for patch types
- No inheritance from parent directories (only explicit references)

---

## 4. Terraform Modules

### Syntax
```hcl
module "network" {
  source = "./modules/network"

  # Explicit inputs (dependency inversion)
  vpc_cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"
}

module "app" {
  source = "./modules/app"

  # Wire outputs to inputs
  vpc_id = module.network.vpc_id
}
```

### Strengths
- Dependency inversion: modules don't embed dependencies
- Explicit data flow via inputs/outputs
- Composition over inheritance
- Flat hierarchy recommended

### Override Semantics
- No implicit inheritance
- All configuration is explicit
- Provider inheritance flows downward

### Weakness
- No "extends" concept
- Verbose for simple cases
- Not applicable for cascading rules

---

## 5. Babel Configuration

### Syntax
```javascript
// babel.config.js
module.exports = {
  extends: "./base.babelrc.js",
  presets: [
    ["@babel/preset-env", { targets: "defaults" }]
  ],
  plugins: [
    "@babel/plugin-transform-runtime"
  ]
};
```

### Strengths
- Simple `extends` with path
- Presets bundle common plugins

### Override Semantics
- Objects merged (not replaced)
- Plugins/presets replaced by identity
- Preset order is reversed (last-to-first)

### Weakness
- Reversed preset order is confusing
- Identity-based replacement is complex
- Known bugs with `extends` + `preset-env`

---

## 6. CSS Cascade Layers

### Syntax
```css
/* Establish layer order first */
@layer reset, framework, components, utilities;

/* Import into named layers */
@import url("normalize.css") layer(reset);
@import url("bootstrap.css") layer(framework);

/* Local overrides in higher-priority layer */
@layer utilities {
  .btn { /* Always wins over framework */ }
}
```

### Nested Layers
```css
@import url("theme.css") layer(framework.themes.dark);
```

### Strengths
- Explicit priority declaration upfront
- Layer order controls cascade (not specificity)
- Dot notation for nesting
- Anonymous layers for isolation

### Override Semantics
- First declared = lowest priority
- Last declared = highest priority (except `!important`)
- Unlayered styles beat all layers

### Weakness
- Browser-only (not general config)
- Must declare order before use
- No directory-based organization

---

## 7. Docker Multi-Stage Builds

### Syntax
```dockerfile
FROM node:20 AS base
RUN npm install

FROM base AS development
CMD ["npm", "run", "dev"]

FROM base AS production
RUN npm run build
CMD ["npm", "start"]
```

### Stage Aliasing
```dockerfile
ARG TARGET=production
FROM alpine:3.18 AS alpine

FROM alpine AS builder
RUN apk add build-base

FROM ${TARGET} AS final
```

### Strengths
- Explicit stage naming with `AS`
- Parallel builds from common base
- ARG-based conditional paths
- COPY --from=stage for artifacts

### Override Semantics
- Each FROM starts fresh
- Only COPY brings artifacts forward
- Conditional via ARG substitution

### Weakness
- Linear inheritance only
- No merge semantics
- Domain-specific (container builds)

---

## Comparative Analysis

| System | Import Syntax | Override Clarity | Directory Support | Flexibility |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------|
| ESLint | `extends: []` | High | Low | High |
| TypeScript | `extends: ""` | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Kustomize | `resources: []` | High | High | High |
| Terraform | `module {}` | Very High | Medium | Medium |
| Babel | `extends: ""` | Low | Low | Low |
| CSS Layers | `@layer` | Very High | N/A | Medium |
| Docker | `FROM AS` | High | N/A | Low |

---

## Recommendations for Agent Rules

### Best Import/Extend Syntax: ESLint's `defineConfig`

```javascript
// Why: Uniform handling of strings, objects, arrays
// No spread operator ambiguity
// File-pattern scoping built-in

defineConfig({
  files: ["src/**"],
  extends: ["./base-rules", localRules, plugin.recommended]
})
```

### Clearest Override Semantics: CSS Cascade Layers

```css
/* Why: Explicit priority declaration upfront */
@layer base, project, user;

/* Order is explicit, not implicit */
```

### Most Flexible Inheritance: Kustomize

```yaml
# Why: Directory-based, surgical patches, components
resources:
  - ../../base
patches:
  - path: override.yaml
components:
  - ../shared-features
```

---

## Proposed Design for Agent Rules

### Directory Structure
```
.claude/
  rules/
    __base__.md           # Global base rules
    security/
      __rules__.md        # Security-specific rules
    frontend/
      __rules__.md        # Frontend-specific rules
      react/
        __rules__.md      # React-specific (inherits from frontend)
```

### Inheritance Syntax (Hybrid Approach)

```yaml
# .claude/rules/frontend/react/__rules__.md
---
extends:
  - ../../__rules__.md    # Inherit from frontend
  - @claude/react         # Built-in preset
priority: 100             # Explicit layer priority
scope:
  files: ["**/*.tsx", "**/*.jsx"]
---

# Rules content in markdown...
```

### Key Design Decisions

1. **From ESLint**: `extends` array with mixed sources
2. **From CSS Layers**: Explicit `priority` number
3. **From Kustomize**: Directory-based organization with `__rules__.md`
4. **From TypeScript**: Node-style resolution for packages

### Override Algorithm

```
1. Collect all __rules__.md from root to current directory
2. Sort by priority (lower = base, higher = override)
3. Merge rules: later wins on conflict
4. Apply file scope filtering
```

### Example Resolution

```
Working on: src/components/Button.tsx

Rules loaded (in order):
1. .claude/rules/__base__.md (priority: 0)
2. .claude/rules/frontend/__rules__.md (priority: 50)
3. .claude/rules/frontend/react/__rules__.md (priority: 100)

Final rules = merge(base, frontend, react)
```

---

## Sources

- [ESLint Flat Config Extends](https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/)
- [TypeScript tsconfig extends](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html)
- [Kustomize Overlays](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/manage-kubernetes-objects/kustomization/)
- [Terraform Module Composition](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/composition)
- [Babel Configuration](https://babeljs.io/docs/configuration)
- [CSS Cascade Layers](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/At-rules/@layer)
- [Docker Multi-stage Builds](https://docs.docker.com/build/building/multi-stage/)

content.text:
## Resumo da Pesquisa: Padrões de Herança de Configuração

Analisei 7 sistemas de configuração modernos. Aqui estão as descobertas principais:

---

### Melhor Sintaxe de Import/Extend: **ESLint Flat Config**

```javascript
import { defineConfig } from "eslint/config";

export default defineConfig({
  files: ["**/*.js"],
  extends: [
    "js/recommended",        // String (preset name)
    pluginConfig,            // Object
    [...arrayOfConfigs]      // Array (auto-flattened)
  ],
  rules: { "no-console": "warn" }  // Override específico
});
```

**Por que funciona:**
- `defineConfig()` elimina confusão com spread operator
- `extends` aceita strings, objetos OU arrays uniformemente
- Escopo por padrão de arquivo embutido

---

### Semântica de Override Mais Clara: **CSS Cascade Layers**

```css
/* Declara ordem de prioridade ANTES do uso */
@layer reset, framework, components, utilities;

@import url("normalize.css") layer(reset);
@import url("bootstrap.css") layer(framework);

@layer utilities {
  .btn { /* Sempre vence o framework, independente da especificidade */ }
}
```

**Por que funciona:**
- Prioridade explícita declarada antecipadamente
- Ordem controla cascade (não especificidade)
- Regras não-layered vencem todas as layers

---

### Herança Mais Flexível: **Kubernetes Kustomize**

```yaml
# overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
resources:
  - ../../base            # Herda do base

patches:
  - path: increase-replicas.yaml
    target:
      kind: Deployment

components:
  - ../../components/caching   # Features reutilizáveis
```

**Por que funciona:**
- Organização baseada em diretórios é cidadã de primeira classe
- Patches são cirúrgicos (merge patch)
- Componentes para features cross-cutting
- Separação explícita base/overlay

---

### Design Proposto para "Agent Rules" em Cascata

```
.claude/
  rules/
    __base__.md           # Regras globais base
    security/
      __rules__.md        # Regras de segurança
    frontend/
      __rules__.md        # Regras frontend
      react/
        __rules__.md      # React-específico (herda de frontend)
```

**Sintaxe híbrida proposta:**

```yaml
# .claude/rules/frontend/react/__rules__.md
---
extends:
  - ../../__rules__.md    # Herança de arquivo (ESLint style)
  - @claude/react         # Preset built-in (TypeScript style)
priority: 100             # Prioridade explícita (CSS Layers style)
scope:
  files: ["**/*.tsx"]     # Escopo por arquivo (ESLint style)
---

# Conteúdo das regras em markdown...
```

**Algoritmo de resolução:**
1. Coleta todos `__rules__.md` da raiz até o diretório atual
2. Ordena por `priority` (menor = base, maior = override)
3. Merge: último vence em conflito
4. Aplica filtro de escopo de arquivo

---

### Tabela Comparativa

| Sistema | Sintaxe | Clareza Override | Suporte Diretório | Flexibilidade |
|---------|---------|-----------------|-------------------|---------------|
| ESLint | `extends: []` | Alta | Baixa | Alta |
| Kustomize | `resources: []` | Alta | Alta | Alta |
| CSS Layers | `@layer` | Muito Alta | N/A | Média |
| TypeScript | `extends: ""` | Média | Baixa | Média |
| Terraform | `module {}` | Muito Alta | Média | Média |
| Docker | `FROM AS` | Alta | N/A | Baixa |
| Babel | `extends: ""` | Baixa | Baixa | Baixa |

---

**Sources:**
- [ESLint Flat Config Extends](https://eslint.org/blog/2025/03/flat-config-extends-define-config-global-ignores/)
- [TypeScript tsconfig extends](https://www.typescriptlang.org/tsconfig/extends.html)
- [Kustomize Overlays](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/manage-kubernetes-objects/kustomization/)
- [Terraform Module Composition](https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/modules/develop/composition)
- [CSS Cascade Layers](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/At-rules/@layer)
- [Docker Multi-stage Builds](https://docs.docker.com/build/building/multi-stage/)
